extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: Thermal incubator system consisting of an insulated styrofoam box with a heatbed, a 0.02 m³/s fan, three DS18S20 1‑Wire temperature sensors, a Raspberry Pi 4 controller, a MOSFET‑based PCB with optocouplers, and a low‑level driver that interfaces the sensors and actuators. The system operates in a laboratory environment to maintain a target temperature for biological samples. Key characteristics include a simple cyber‑physical architecture, real‑time thermal dynamics, open‑source hardware and software, and a modular design that facilitates digital twinning.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Real‑time monitoring and visualization of temperature and actuator states via RabbitMQ messages and a time‑series database; on‑demand calibration of the thermal model (Model B) using collected data; anomaly detection and heat‑bed state estimation through a Kalman filter; what‑if simulation via co‑simulation of the physical twin and digital twin models; a self‑adaptation loop that detects changes, schedules experiments, recalibrates the model, re‑optimizes controller parameters, and updates the controller.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Hardware: Raspberry Pi 4, DS18S20 1‑Wire temperature sensors, 0.02 m³/s fan, MOSFETs, optocouplers, custom PCB. Software: RabbitMQ message broker, low‑level driver and controller (Python), Kalman filter implementation, Model B thermal simulation (four‑parameter model), calibration scripts, co‑simulation framework, time‑series database (e.g., InfluxDB), dashboard (e.g., Grafana). All components are open‑source and hosted on GitHub (https://github.com/INTO‑CPS‑Association/example‑incubator).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The digital twin operates asynchronously via a RabbitMQ message broker. The low‑level driver periodically polls the DS18S20 temperature sensors and publishes the readings to RabbitMQ; the controller subscribes to these messages, processes the data, and publishes actuator commands (fan and heatbed control) back to RabbitMQ. No explicit polling interval, synchronization frequency, or latency requirements are specified in the documents; the temporal granularity is therefore message‑based rather than time‑synchronised.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The case study implements a single physical twin (the incubator) and a corresponding digital twin composed of several logical components (controller, low‑level driver, Kalman filter, what‑if simulation engine). There is no mention of multiple twin instances or a hierarchical twin‑of‑twin structure. The architecture is centralized: all components run on the same Raspberry Pi (controller, driver) or on a local machine that subscribes to RabbitMQ messages. No decentralized or distributed twin instances are described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The DT is orchestrated around a RabbitMQ message broker that serves as the central communication backbone. The physical side consists of a Raspberry Pi 4 running the controller and low‑level driver, which read DS18S20 sensors and drive a MOSFET‑controlled heatbed and fan. The digital side comprises: (1) a real‑time dashboard that subscribes to RabbitMQ topics to plot temperature and actuator states; (2) an on‑demand calibration module that triggers a calibration routine (Model B) when requested; (3) a Kalman‑filter module that estimates the unmeasured heatbed temperature using Model B and the sensor data; (4) a what‑if simulation engine that performs co‑simulation using the calibrated model; and (5) a self‑adaptation loop that, upon detecting anomalies, schedules experiments, re‑calibrates, and re‑configures the controller. Resource management is implicit: all components share the same RabbitMQ broker and the local network; the controller and driver are CPU‑bound on the Pi, while the simulation and Kalman filter run on a separate host. Coordination is achieved through message topics and a simple state machine defined in the controller’s statechart.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The system integrates with external systems only through the RabbitMQ broker, which is a standard open‑source message‑broker supporting AMQP. No other external protocols (e.g., MQTT, OPC UA) are mentioned. The repository on GitHub provides the source code and configuration, but no integration with enterprise ERP, MES, or cloud services is described. Therefore, horizontal integration is limited to the local RabbitMQ broker and the GitHub repository for code distribution.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin constellation for the incubator comprises two analytical thermal models and a suite of data‑driven algorithms.  Model A is a two‑parameter model with free parameters C_air (J K⁻¹) and G_box (J K⁻¹) that captures the bulk heat capacity and heat loss of the box.  Model B extends this with two additional parameters – C_heater (J K⁻¹) and G_heater (J K⁻¹) – to represent the thermal inertia of the heatbed itself.  Both models are calibrated against a data trajectory collected during a dedicated calibration experiment (see Document 5).  The data stream consists of temperature readings from three sensors (T1, T2, T3), fan voltage (12 V or 18 V), heatbed power, and timestamps.  Data are transmitted from the low‑level driver to a RabbitMQ message broker, then ingested into a time‑series database for real‑time visualization.  Algorithms operating on this data include a bang‑bang controller (with LL, UL, H, C parameters), a Kalman filter for anomaly detection and heatbed state estimation, and a calibration routine that re‑estimates the free parameters of Model B on demand.  The DT framework therefore integrates model‑based prediction, data acquisition, and algorithmic decision‑making to maintain and optimise the physical twin.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation is performed by a fan and a heatbed heater, both controlled through a low‑level driver running on a Raspberry Pi.  The controller implements a bang‑bang logic: when the measured temperature falls below the lower limit (LL) it turns the heatbed on for a heating duration (H); after reaching the upper limit (UL) it waits for a cooling duration (C) before re‑evaluating.  Fan voltage is set to either 12 V or 18 V depending on the experiment.  Remote control is achieved via RabbitMQ messages; the controller publishes control commands and subscribes to sensor updates.  Safety constraints are enforced by a safe protocol in the low‑level driver that limits actuation ranges, enforces waiting periods after each actuation to avoid overheating, and ensures that the PT remains within safe operating limits.  Addressing is handled by RabbitMQ routing keys that distinguish between controller‑to‑driver and driver‑to‑controller traffic.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing infrastructure comprises three temperature sensors: T1 positioned outside the incubator to measure ambient room temperature, T2 placed at the hottest point inside the box, and T3 at the coldest point.  Sensors are read periodically by the low‑level driver and the data are forwarded to RabbitMQ.  While the exact sensor type (thermistor, RTD, etc.) is not specified, the system records temperature in degrees Celsius with a sampling interval sufficient to capture the thermal dynamics (on the order of seconds).  Spatial distribution is deliberately asymmetric to capture temperature gradients; the average of T2 and T3 is used as the internal air temperature estimate.  Data transmission rates are governed by the RabbitMQ broker, which delivers messages with minimal latency to support real‑time monitoring and control.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is evaluated by comparing simulated temperature trajectories against measured data from the calibration experiment.  Model B, with four free parameters, demonstrates superior fidelity in capturing the heatbed’s thermal inertia.  Validation is performed through on‑demand calibration: the system automatically runs a parameter estimation routine that fits C_air, G_box, C_heater, and G_heater to the observed data.  Verification is supported by a Kalman filter that continuously estimates the unmeasured heatbed temperature and flags anomalies.  Uncertainty is implicitly quantified by the residuals of the calibration fit; error detection is achieved by monitoring deviations between model predictions and sensor readings.  Quality assurance includes real‑time visualization dashboards, periodic calibration, and a self‑adaptation loop that triggers re‑calibration and controller re‑configuration when anomalies are detected.  These processes collectively ensure that both the models and the underlying data maintain high fidelity and validity throughout the DT lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Temperature data is collected from three DS18S20 1‑Wire digital thermometers (accuracy ±0.5 °C, range –55 °C to +125 °C) by the low‑level driver running on the Raspberry Pi 4 Model B. The driver periodically polls the sensors (polling interval not specified in the documents) and publishes the raw temperature readings to a local RabbitMQ message broker via the AMQP protocol. The data is transmitted as messages to the communication server, which serves as the interface between the physical twin (PT) and the digital twin (DT). No explicit data preprocessing, quality‑assurance, or integration mechanisms beyond the sensor accuracy and the message broker are described.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands are issued from the DT to the PT through the RabbitMQ broker. The controller component, also running on the Raspberry Pi, subscribes to command queues and receives control messages (e.g., turn heatbed on/off, fan on/off). These messages are forwarded by the low‑level driver to the PT via the GPIO pins, which drive MOSFETs and optocouplers to actuate the heatbed and fan. The low‑level driver incorporates a safe‑protocol to ensure that the PT remains in a safe state; however, the specific validation, authentication, conflict‑resolution, or execution‑confirmation mechanisms are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: All communication between the DT components is carried over RabbitMQ, an AMQP 0‑9‑1 compliant message broker running on the same Raspberry Pi 4. The controller and the low‑level driver act as AMQP clients, publishing and subscribing to queues that carry sensor data and control commands. No explicit network topology beyond the local host is described; the system relies on the reliability features of RabbitMQ (message persistence and acknowledgements) but does not specify additional redundancy or security mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The DT is deployed entirely on a single Raspberry Pi 4 Model B. The Raspberry Pi hosts the RabbitMQ broker, the controller, the low‑level driver, and a local time‑series database used for real‑time visualization of the incubator status. All components run on the Pi’s onboard resources; no external cloud or edge servers are used. The deployment is on‑premise, with the Pi acting as both the physical twin controller and the digital twin host.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The digital twin for the incubator is developed in a sequence of clearly defined phases that mirror the typical CPS development lifecycle. 1) **Conceptual modelling** – two thermal models (Model A with two free parameters and Model B with four free parameters) are formulated to capture the heatbed and box dynamics. 2) **Calibration and validation** – Model B is calibrated against experimental data to better predict the heatbed’s thermal inertia; on‑demand calibration is planned to be triggered automatically during operation. 3) **Integration** – the physical twin (heatbed, fan, sensors, Raspberry Pi controller) is wired to a low‑level driver that publishes sensor data to a RabbitMQ communication server; the controller implements a bang‑bang logic with parameters LL, UL, H, C. 4) **Deployment** – the software stack (RabbitMQ, controller, driver) is deployed on the Raspberry Pi and the data are streamed to a time‑series database for real‑time dashboard visualization. 5) **Operational monitoring** – a Kalman filter runs on the digital twin to estimate the unmeasured heatbed temperature and detect anomalies. 6) **Evolution / self‑adaptation** – a closed‑loop self‑adaptation routine is defined: when an anomaly is detected, an experiment is scheduled, new data are collected, Model B is re‑calibrated, the Kalman filter is re‑configured, what‑if simulations are run to re‑optimize controller parameters, and the controller is updated. 7) **Continuous improvement** – the loop above allows the twin to evolve with changing plant behaviour, ensuring the digital twin remains an accurate, up‑to‑date representation of the physical incubator.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

**Low-level driver** – Abstracts the low level communication with the sensors and actu
ators.

**Controller** – Implements the control logic required to regulate the temperature inside
the incubator. It receives the measured data and sends commands through the
communication server.


**2.2.1** **The Communication Server**


For the case study, the RabbitMQ [4] server was deployed for synchronisation and communication. With tens of thousands of users, RabbitMQ is one of the most popular
open source message brokers. RabbitMQ is a mature, lightweight, and easy to deploy
on premises and in the cloud.

The controller on the Raspberry Pi is listening from the RabbitMQ server for commands of controlling the fan and the heatbed and at the same time sharing the control
states to the RabbitMQ server.
We choose RabbitMQ because it greatly facilitates the digital twinning process, as
the DT can be implemented by components that listen to the messages being passed
between the controller and the load level driver, as illustrated in fig. 7.


**2.2.2** **The Low-Level Driver**


The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

to the RabbitMQ server. In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is
incorporated into the low-level driver.
The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are
commands from the controller, through the communication server. The temperature
data is uploaded to the communication server.


**2.2.3** **The Controller**


The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the

The dynamics model we created mainly focuses on predicting the temperature inside

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary** We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. **2.1.3** **The Fan** We installed a fan (see fig. 3(c)) inside the box to circulate the air inside the box for making the temperature as uniformly distributed as possible. The fan airflow is about 0 _._ 02m [3] s _[−]_ [1] . With a box volume of 0 _._ 03m [3], the fan should take 2 seconds to move all the air within the box. **2.1.4** **The Raspberry Pi** A Raspberry Pi 4 Model B (see fig. 3(d)) was selected to implement the controller. The General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) connectors on the Raspberry Pi can be used to connect many accessories such as temperature sensors, fan, and the heatbed. Furthermore it supports wireless internet out of the box, with built-in Wi-Fi and Bluetooth, 5 which simplifies deployment of the controller. **2.1.5** **The Temperature Sensor** It is necessary to get feedback from the temperature sensors for feedback control. To do this, a DS18S20 High-Precision 1-Wire Digital Thermometer (see fig. 3(e)) was used for measuring the temperature. This temperature sensor can measure temperatures from -55 °C to +125 °C with an accuracy of 0.5 °C from -10 to +85 °C, which is suitable for the physical incubator. And each DS18S20 has a unique 64-bit serial code, which allows multiple DS18S20s to function on the same 1-Wire bus. Such 1-wire bus support to connect multiple sensors with only one GPIO. In the incubator system, three sensors were deployed for detecting the temperature. **2.1.6** **The PCB Design** Controlling the heatbed requires approximately 4A current which is not supported by the Raspberry Pi. Instead, MOSFETs are used to separate the control signals from the Raspberry Pi and the driving currents. Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative

develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

directly be controlled.

wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

We choose RabbitMQ because it greatly facilitates the digital twinning process, as
the DT can be implemented by components that listen to the messages being passed

**2.2.2** **The Low-Level Driver**


The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

to the RabbitMQ server. In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is
incorporated into the low-level driver.
The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are
commands from the controller, through the communication server. The temperature
data is uploaded to the communication server.


**2.2.3** **The Controller**


The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the
